title: Talent Hive
tagline: Shortlist hundreds of resumes with a custom-trained NLP engine — built for HR teams who'd rather read three good candidates than skim three hundred bad ones.
aboutHeadline: A resume-shortlisting engine wrapped in the UI HR teams actually want to use.
blurb: Talent Hive is a two-sided hiring platform: HR managers post jobs and surface top candidates through a custom-trained NLP scoring engine, while candidates apply with a single resume upload that auto-fills every form field they'd otherwise have to type by hand. The scoring engine is a SpaCy-based NER model with cosine-similarity matching against job descriptions, served over FastAPI as a sidecar to the main Node app. Trust is built into the UI: every auto-extracted field is editable, every score is explainable, and HRMs can see which keywords drove the ranking.
pullQuotes: The model proposes. The HR manager — and the candidate — get the final word.
An NLP engine is only as good as the moment the HR manager decides to trust it.
Built for the people who actually do the hiring, not the people who write about it.

highlights.title: Custom-trained resume parsing & scoring
highlights.lede: A SpaCy NER pipeline trained on hundreds of resumes.
highlights.body: Resumes are uploaded to S3, parsed by a custom-trained SpaCy NER model that extracts skills, experience, and education spans, then scored against the job description using cosine similarity over weighted keyword sets. The whole pipeline runs as a FastAPI service so the React app stays thin.

shots.caption: Spans extracted, then scored against the JD.
HRMs see which keywords drove the ranking.

highlights.title: HR dashboard & shortlist flow
highlights.lede: Post a job, watch the shortlist build itself.
highlights.body: HR managers create jobs, preview the candidate-facing posting, and watch applicants flow into a ranked queue as they apply. Accept, reject, or schedule an interview directly from the candidate row — no email back-and-forth required.

shots.caption: Preview matches what the candidate sees.
Accept, reject, or schedule in one click.

highlights.title: Frictionless candidate apply
highlights.lede: Upload a resume. Watch the form fill itself.
highlights.body: Candidates upload a resume once and the same parsing engine auto-fills every field of every job application — experience, education, skills — with each entry editable, addable, and removable. Trust comes from giving the user the last word on what the model extracted.

highlights.title: Company & role management
highlights.body: Companies have multiple HRMs with role-scoped permissions, branding controls, and audit trails. Admins manage who can post, who can shortlist, and who sees candidate data — modeled in Mongo with the same access checks the UI uses.

challengesHeadline: Where the model met the messy world.

challenges.title: Parsing resumes that don't look like resumes
challenges.body: Real resumes are PDFs full of multi-column layouts, embedded tables, ligatures, and 'creative' typography. The parser had to survive all of it. Aggressive pre-processing (column detection, ligature normalization, OCR fallback for image-based PDFs) and a NER model trained on a deliberately ugly dataset got the accuracy to a place where HRMs trusted what came out the other side.

challenges.title: Letting the candidate override the model without breaking the score
challenges.body: When a candidate edits an auto-extracted field, the cosine-similarity score has to update — but only on the fields the candidate confirmed. The pipeline splits 'extracted' from 'confirmed' state in Mongo, and the scoring engine only weights confirmed spans. The candidate stays in control; the score stays honest.

challenges.title: Designing an explainable shortlist, not a black box
challenges.body: An unexplained ranking is just a vibe. The candidate view surfaces the keywords that drove the match score and the spans they came from, so an HRM can sanity-check the model in seconds rather than trust it on faith. The lesson was that explainability isn't a feature you add — it's the reason the model gets used at all.

soft: Product framing of an ML model
